transcript: Of course, there is also a 100% chance of fireworks in the sky tonight across Central Texas. But if you want to shoot those fireworks yourself, where can you legally do that? Channel 6's Andrew Moore bought some fireworks this morning and has been looking for a spot to fire them off all day long. Andrew, any luck? Fourth of July means great food and great fireworks. But where can you set these off? Well, let's go grab some and find out. We've got artillery shells that you know you can have a tube and you can reload them cities don't normally allow fireworks and while we checked with temple to be sure it turns out you can't even have them in the city with the government-owned leg just down the road i decided to see if that was an option surely Surely the park ranger will think the lake is safe for fireworks, right? Cruels and regulations, which is a title 36, which governs all core property, does prohibit the possession of fireworks. Darn. But did the ranger have any suggestions? Private property outside the city limits, and of course with the landowner's permission. Of course not everyone knows a person out in the county but were there any public county spots? I couldn't find any place designated for fireworks in Bell County but what about public rest stops? Well turns out according to the Texas Department of Public Safety a county can choose to prohibit fireworks at rest stops, but they don't have to. And that's when I found a rest stop in Salado without any signs saying you can't light them. And that means... So if you're looking to set off fireworks, a local rest stop might be your best bet. Just make sure there are no signs that prohibit fireworks in the area. And of course, don't mess with Texas. Clean up afterwards. In bell county andrew moore channel 6 news
